name: Irregular Verbs: sein, haben & stem changes
summary: The two most important irregular verbs, plus vowel-changing verbs you meet every day.
explanation:
Two verbs power most German sentences and both are irregular: 'sein' (to be) and 'haben' (to have). They must be memorised because they do not follow the regular pattern.

A large group of common verbs also changes its stem vowel in the du and er/sie/es forms only. 'fahren' (to drive) becomes 'du fährst, er fährt' (a to a); 'sprechen' (to speak) becomes 'du sprichst, er spricht' (e to i); 'lesen' (to read) becomes 'du liest, er liest' (e to ie).

The endings stay regular; only the stem vowel shifts, and only in those two forms.
pattern: sein/haben: memorise  |  stem-change verbs: vowel shifts in du & er/sie/es

examples.en: We are hungry. (literally: we have hunger)
He is driving to Munich. (a to a)
Do you speak English? (e to i)
She is reading a book. (e to ie)

commonMistakes: Regularising sein: it is 'ich bin', never 'ich seine'.
Saying 'Ich bin Hunger'. German uses haben: 'Ich habe Hunger'.
Applying the stem change to ich, wir, ihr, sie. It only happens in du and er/sie/es.
Forgetting the vowel change altogether: it is 'er spricht', not 'er sprecht'.
tip: If a verb changes its stem vowel, it does so only in du and er/sie/es. The other four forms look completely regular.
